data.title: OGE Energy outlines 5%–7% EPS growth target through 2028 as transmission, data center demand drive long-term investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OGE Energy Corp. (OGE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO R. Trauschke highlighted that "2025 was another strong year and a continuation of the momentum we are building and setting the foundation for a long runway of future growth with generation and transmission opportunities." He reported consolidated earnings of $2.32 per share for the year, including $2.47 per share at the electric company and a holding company loss of $0.15. Trauschke emphasized delivery in the top half of guidance, filing for recovery of generation needs, securing financing for long-term growth, and leveraging local economic strength for job growth and investment.
  • Trauschke announced the execution of a well-subscribed equity offering, a filing for generation preapproval of the 300-megawatt Frontier Energy Storage project, and the issuance of two RFPs and a draft 2026 IRP. He stated, "We will advance our transmission strategy and finalize the opportunities from the SPP ITP, recognizing its critical role in reliability and its growing contribution to long-term investment opportunities."
  • The CEO revealed the company is finalizing a 1 gigawatt contract with a data center customer, referred to as customer X, and plans to file a large load tariff by midyear. He reassured that "our priority remains protecting residential customers, and we have built explicit consumer protection measures into that framework."
  • CFO Charles Walworth stated, "We delivered another strong year in 2025, finishing at the upper end of our original guidance range, and we're entering 2026 with solid momentum." Walworth cited consolidated net income for 2025 at approximately $471 million or $2.32 per diluted share compared to $442 million or $2.19 in 2024, driven by recovery of capital investments and strong load growth.

Outlook

  • The company is guiding to consolidated earnings of $2.43 per share in 2026, with a range of $2.38 to $2.48, which Walworth noted "represents a 7% increase from the 2025 midpoint." OGE set a long-term EPS growth target of 5%–7% off this higher starting point and expects to deliver in the top half of the range in 2027 and 2028.
  • Customer count is expected to increase about 1% and weather-normalized load to grow 4% to 6% in 2026. The company plans to file a rate review midyear in Oklahoma and may evaluate the timing of an Arkansas rate review later in the year.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net income for 2025 was approximately $471 million or $2.32 per diluted share, up from $442 million or $2.19 per share in 2024. Electric company net income reached approximately $500 million or $2.47 per share, compared to $470 million or $2.33 per share the prior year, attributed to capital investment recovery and strong load growth. The holding company reported a loss of $29 million or $0.15 per share, slightly higher year-over-year due to increased interest expense, partially offset by a onetime legacy midstream benefit.
  • Customer growth was just under 1% and weather-normalized load grew approximately 7% for 2025.
  • The company expects to issue approximately $300 million of debt at the electric utility in 2026 and targets a 60% to 70% dividend payout ratio with a stable and growing dividend.

Q&A

  • Whitney Mutalemwa, Wells Fargo, asked about the linkage between the investment plan and rate base growth. Walworth responded, "the plan as laid out on that slide equates to 9%."
  • Mutalemwa queried changes in the large load panel. Trauschke said, "we still are in active negotiations with 6 to 7 large load customers in various stages" and highlighted nearing conclusion for customer X.
  • Brian Russo, Jefferies, questioned the moderation of weather-normalized load growth. Walworth explained, "these loads are not always super, super steady and that there's some ebb and flow to that... we've averaged about 5%."
  • Russo also asked about the termination of the Black Kettle Energy Storage capacity purchase. Trauschke replied, "we've been a strong proponent of being the owner and the operator of these assets... it just further validates that thesis."
  • Aditya Gandhi, Wolfe Research, inquired about OGE's portion of the Seminole-to-Shreveport 765 kV line. Walworth estimated, "I see it as probably something that's on the order of 20% of our current capital plan", but noted this is preliminary.
  • Gandhi also asked about customer protections in the large load tariff. Walworth said protections ensure "that large customer pays its fair share, has minimum terms, collateral requirements, all those types of things that you would expect."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was largely positive but probing, with recurring questions about load growth sustainability, timing of major contract announcements, capital allocation, and regulatory impacts. Repeated focus on customer X and large load pipeline reflects investor interest in long-term growth drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, using phrases like "we remain confident in our financial plan" and "we reliably deliver results." In Q&A, both Trauschke and Walworth provided detailed responses, signaling transparency and steady execution. There was no notable defensiveness or hesitancy compared to the previous quarter, and management reiterated their growth track record and prudent approach.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted slightly toward more granular scrutiny of capital plan execution and contract timing, while management retained a consistent, confident stance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from confidence in delivering at the "top half of guidance" in Q3 to specifying a 7% EPS growth target for 2026 and setting a long-term 5%–7% EPS growth framework through 2028.
  • Strategic focus expanded from generation additions and regulatory filings (Q3) to a broader set of transmission and large customer contracts, including explicit details on the Frontier Energy Storage project and major transmission assignments.
  • Analyst focus moved from generalized load growth and CapEx timing to detailed questions on the linkage between investments and rate base growth, large load contract progress, and the implication of project terminations.
  • Management's confidence level remained strong but was supported by more concrete forward guidance and project milestones. Analysts' tone became more probing on execution details and future contract disclosures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management referenced the need to balance affordability with execution, especially as growth catalysts such as new transmission projects and large customer contracts are layered into the plan.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing and mechanics of rate base growth, the potential impact of major project terminations, and the ability to manage dilution and CapEx alongside earnings growth.
  • Management stated they are actively managing capital prioritization and will update investment plans as regulatory approvals are received and projects are finalized.

Final Takeaway

OGE Energy Corp. management emphasized a strong finish to 2025 and signaled a clear path for continued earnings growth, setting a 7% EPS target for 2026 and a 5%–7% long-term growth framework, underpinned by robust load growth, transmission assignments, and a near-term finalization of a major data center contract. The company remains focused on affordability, prudent capital allocation, and delivering reliable returns to shareholders, with multiple catalysts and investment opportunities ahead as regulatory approvals and project milestones are reached.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nano-cap biotech Sensei surges on $200M private placement, acquisition
data.description:
• Shares of Sensei Biotherapeutics (SNSE) surged on Wednesday after the nano-cap biotech announced a transaction to acquire Faeth Therapeutics, a cancer drug developer, alongside a private placement for approximately $200M.
  • The acquisition of San Francisco-based Faeth adds an oral cancer therapy known as PIKTOR to Sensei’s (SNSE) pipeline.
  • Concurrently, the company announced it would issue Series B non-voting convertible preferred stock to investors, including RA Capital Management, as part of a private placement seeking ~$200M in gross proceeds.
  • Sensei (SNSE) plans to use its proceeds mainly to advance PIKTOR through multiple catalysts expected by the end of this year, including a topline readout from a mid-stage trial in endometrial cancer and the start of an early-stage trial in breast cancer.

data.title: MKS Inc. signals low 20% year-over-year growth in electronics & packaging for Q1 2026 amid strong AI-driven demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MKS Inc. (MKSI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Lee, President and CEO, stated, "2025 was a year of impressive execution for MKS in a gradually improving demand environment. Year-over-year, we delivered 10% sales growth, 20% EPS growth and over 20% free cash flow growth." He emphasized the company's strong gross margins and focus on customer delivery, business investment, and proactive leverage reduction. Lee also highlighted, "Our Q4 revenue, gross margin and earnings per diluted share all came in above the midpoint of the guidance ranges we provided on our Q3 call in November. Revenue was strong across all three of our end markets."
  • Lee reported strong semiconductor market performance, with revenue above the high end of guidance, driven by subsystems for etch and deposition in DRAM and logic foundry markets. He noted, "Our plasma and reactive gases business delivered another strong quarter. We also maintained healthy momentum in dissolved gases for advanced logic applications and in back-end applications related to high bandwidth memory."
  • Lee announced the ramping of a new supercenter factory in Malaysia in the second half of the year, which will add capacity and resiliency. In Electronics & Packaging, he cited a sequential revenue increase primarily from flexible PCB drilling and chemistry equipment sales and noted, "Excluding the impact of FX and palladium pass-through, the chemistry sales increased 16% in the fourth quarter and 11% for the full year compared to the same periods in 2024." He linked ongoing growth to AI-driven complexity in packaging.
  • On specialty industrial markets, Lee stated, "revenues came in at the high end of our guidance. We saw sequential improvement in research and defense in certain industrial applications."
  • Ramakumar Mayampurath, EVP & CFO, said, "We ended the year with a very strong fourth quarter. Demand increased across all three end markets. We delivered healthy margins, robust free cash flow and made meaningful progress on our deleveraging goals."
  • Mayampurath reported, "MKS reported revenue of $1.03 billion, up 5% sequentially and 10% year-over-year. Fourth quarter semiconductor revenue was $435 million, up 5% sequentially and 9% year-over-year."
  • Mayampurath also highlighted, "Fourth quarter net earnings of $168 million or $2.47 per diluted share which is above the midpoint of our guidance."

Outlook

  • For Q1 2026, Mayampurath guided, "we expect revenue of $1.04 billion, plus or minus $40 million." By end market, "semiconductor market is expected to be $150 million, plus or minus $15 million. Revenue from electronics and packaging market is expected to be $305 million, plus or minus $15 million and revenue from our specialty industrial market is expected to be $285 million, plus or minus $10 million."
  • Lee projected, "Looking ahead to Q1 and the anticipated seasonal impact from the Luna New Year holiday, we expect electronics and packaging revenue to be up slightly sequentially and to increase in the low 20% range year-over-year."
  • Mayampurath forecasted, "we estimate first quarter gross margin of 4% to 6% plus or minus 100 basis points. We expect first quarter operating expenses of $270 million plus or minus $5 million."
  • No comparison to analysts' estimates was included as no sufficient data was provided.

Financial Results

  • Mayampurath provided that, "Fourth quarter Electronics & Packaging revenue was $303 million, an increase of 5% quarter-over-quarter and 19% year-over-year. Chemistry sales in the quarter were up 16% year-over-year, excluding the impact of FX and palladium pass-through."
  • Specialty industrial revenue was $295 million, a 4% sequential increase and 5% year-over-year rise.
  • Fourth quarter gross margin was 46.4%. Operating expenses were $263 million. Operating income was approximately $217 million, yielding an operating margin of 21%. Adjusted EBITDA was $249 million with a 24.1% margin. Net interest expenses were $42 million. The effective tax rate was 1%. Net debt at year-end was $3.6 billion.
  • For the full year, revenue was $3.9 billion, semiconductor revenue totaled $1.7 billion, and Electronics & Packaging revenue was $1.1 billion. Free cash flow for the year was $497 million, up 21% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Steve Barger, KeyBanc: Asked about the 46% gross margin guide and chemistry equipment mix. Mayampurath responded, "It is due to the seasonality from lower chemistry driven by Lunar New Year and we expect the mix to improve in Q2 and further in Q3."
  • Barger, KeyBanc: Questioned the impact of memory shortages and NAND investments. Lee explained, "The industry is moving very fast to try to meet those demands...NAND has become potentially a bottleneck as well...We have plenty of capacity to meet those upgrades should they come."
  • James Ricchiuti, Needham: Queried about the proportion of Electronics & Packaging growth due to capacity additions. Lee described chemistry as more "utilization dependent" and said "the rest of that growth is capacity additions from chemistry equipment as well as flex drilling equipment."
  • Melissa Weathers, Deutsche Bank: Sought views on WFE growth and AI offsetting consumer electronics slowness. Lee said, "WFE will be a large grower," and noted increasing PCB layer counts for AI applications, stating, "AI, we're talking about doubling the number of layers."
  • Michael Mani, BofA Securities: Asked about capacity and the Malaysia facility. Lee replied, "Malaysia is...future capacity needs for WFE. We always build our factories in phases."
  • Mani, BofA: Followed up on chemistry revenue sensitivity. Lee maintained the $20 million to $40 million per $100 million equipment sales model and emphasized the time lag between equipment sales and chemistry revenue.
  • Shane Brett, Morgan Stanley: Asked if chemistry revenue would accelerate in 2026. Lee referenced seasonality, stating AI chemistry will "more than make up" for any weakness in consumer product chemistry.
  • David Liu, Mizuho: Asked about the timing of hitting a "5 handle" for semis revenue. Lee replied, "5 handle would not be surprising."
  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: Questioned the lower sequential growth guide compared to main customers. Lee stated, "when that ramp occurs, it just accelerates fast. This is our best view today."
  • Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: Asked about gross margin leverage. Mayampurath confirmed, "We are very pleased with the gross margin for 2025. And if it weren't for tariffs, you would have been to your point or 47%."
  • James A. Schreiner: Inquired about production constraints. Lee responded, "During the beginning of the ramp, we're never the constraint...we have plant capacity. The challenge is getting the supply chain to ramp up."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the sustainability of growth in chemistry and equipment, the impact of AI on revenue, margin trajectory, and potential constraints, reflecting a slightly positive to neutral tone with some skepticism about cyclical and seasonal variability.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, frequently using phrases such as "we are confident" and "we have plenty of capacity," and highlighting resilience and readiness to meet customer demand, as well as ongoing investments in capacity and deleveraging. During Q&A, management responses were clear and detailed without signs of deflection or defensiveness.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was slightly more optimistic regarding AI-driven demand, while analysts continued to probe for sustainability and potential bottlenecks, showing a consistent level of scrutiny.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 guidance and outlook commentary signaled a higher year-over-year growth expectation for Electronics & Packaging (low 20% range vs. prior double-digit guidance).
  • Management highlighted the ramping of a new Malaysia facility, whereas in the prior quarter, focus was on factory capacity expansion within existing sites.
  • Analysts continued to focus on chemistry revenue visibility and potential acceleration, similar to previous calls, but were more insistent on the role of AI in offsetting consumer weakness.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, EPS, and free cash flow all showed sequential and year-over-year improvement, with a particular emphasis on robust cash flow generation and debt reduction.
  • Management's confidence around supply chain and capacity constraints improved, referencing better preparedness for potential ramps.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing impacts from tariffs, higher palladium prices, and product mix effects, specifically noting that tariffs continue to impact gross margins by approximately 50 basis points.
  • Automotive segment softness persisted within specialty industrial, with management noting only modest improvement across other segments.
  • Seasonal factors, especially the Lunar New Year, were referenced as impacting chemistry and electronics revenues in Q1.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential cyclicality in consumer electronics and the risk of supply chain bottlenecks during rapid ramps.

Final Takeaway

MKS Inc. management emphasized a strong finish to 2025, with robust growth across all key end markets and notable momentum in AI-driven electronics and packaging demand. The company is positioning for further growth in 2026, supported by ongoing investments in capacity, particularly with the new Malaysia facility, and continued focus on deleveraging and operational flexibility. Management expects AI-related complexity to drive further gains despite macro and seasonal headwinds, and remains confident in both the company’s ability to meet demand and its long-term growth trajectory.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: LCI Industries outlines $4.2B–$4.3B 2026 revenue target while expanding consolidation and margin initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LCI Industries (LCII) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jason Lippert, CEO, opened by highlighting "strong results as our team continues to execute effectively, delivering a 16% year-over-year top line growth, along with further margin expansion in the fourth quarter." Lippert reported OEM segment net sales increased 18% to $737 million, with RV OEM revenue up 17%, attributing growth to market share gains, new product sales, and a favorable shift toward higher content units. The company expects to expand market share across all four OEM markets, emphasizing innovation as a central growth driver.
  • Lippert noted, "our five most recently launched products are now generating an annualized revenue run rate of approximately $225 million," and highlighted the success of the Chill Cube air conditioner platform and the launch of the Sundeck patio system.
  • Market share gains and content growth in North American utility trailer, bus, and marine OEM customers drove 21% year-over-year net sales growth in those areas, with bus-related content contributing $31 million. Integration of Freedman Seating and Trans Air is ahead of plan.
  • In the aftermarket, net sales grew 8% to $196 million, supported by product innovations and growing repair cycle demand. Lippert described investments in service infrastructure, including new service facilities and an expanded mobile technician workforce, resulting in a double-digit increase in service completions.
  • Lippert addressed a $50 million annual opportunity from a competitor's bankruptcy in the auto aftermarket, with existing capacity positioned to absorb incremental business. The company also transitioned to a 600,000-square-foot distribution center and announced a new manufacturing facility for Ranch Hands truck accessories.
  • Lippert stated, "we delivered a full year operating margin of 6.8%, an improvement of 100 basis points year-over-year, driven by cost improvements, market share gains and enhanced operating efficiencies."
  • Lippert reported returning $243 million to shareholders in 2025, with $114 million in dividends and $129 million in share repurchases. He closed by expressing confidence in the company's strategy, stating, "I've never been more excited about the opportunities in front of us than I am today."
  • CFO Lillian Etzkorn stated, "we ended the year on a strong note with the fourth quarter results that included double-digit top line growth and meaningful margin expansion."

Outlook

  • Etzkorn provided guidance for 2026, expecting RV wholesale shipments of 335,000 to 350,000 units and marine industry performance to be flat to up low single digits. Transportation is projected to be flat, with growth from Freedman Seating and Trans Air acquisitions. Housing is expected to grow in the low single digits, aided by residential window product expansion.
  • Aftermarket growth is forecasted at mid-single digits, supported by a surge in RVs entering the repair cycle. Automotive aftermarket sales are anticipated to benefit from competitor disruption.
  • Etzkorn reported, "we expect consolidated 2026 revenue of $4.2 billion to $4.3 billion, and operating margin in the range of 7.5% to 8% and adjusted diluted EPS of $8.25 to $9.25."
  • The company plans to consolidate 8 to 10 facilities in 2026, continue efficiency initiatives, and pursue divestitures of lower-margin noncore products. Capital expenditures are expected to be $60 million to $80 million, with ongoing share repurchases and dividends.

Financial Results

  • Etzkorn reported fourth quarter consolidated net sales of $933 million, up 16% year-over-year. OEM net sales grew 18%, with RVs up 17%. Content per towable RV unit increased 11% to $5,670, and content per motorized unit rose 7% to $3,993.
  • Aftermarket net sales reached $196 million, an 8% increase. Consolidated operating profit was $35 million, with a margin of 3.8%. OEM-related operating profit margin was 3.7%. Aftermarket operating profit margin was 4.3%, down from 7.9% a year earlier, with margin pressure attributed to higher material costs, tariffs, and investments in capacity and distribution.
  • Adjusted EBITDA rose 53% to $70 million, with a 7.5% margin. GAAP net income was $19 million or $0.77 per diluted share, and adjusted net income was $22 million or $0.89 per share. Cash and cash equivalents ended the year at $223 million, with net debt at $723 million and a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.8x.
  • January net sales were approximately $343 million, up 4% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Patrick Buckley, Jefferies: Asked about sensitivity of 2026 outlook to rate cuts and drivers of guidance range. Lippert responded that "we're not factoring the rate cuts into the range," and growth is driven by market share gains.
  • Buckley, Jefferies: Sought clarity on the "normal run rate" for shipments. Lippert explained the industry is "coming out slow," estimating a near-term normalized range of 375,000 to 415,000 units.
  • Scott Stember, ROTH: Inquired about retail trends, aftermarket performance, and Q1 cadence. Lippert noted larger dealers are "doing okay" while small/midsize dealers are "struggling," and Etzkorn indicated January's 4% sales increase is indicative for Q1.
  • Dan Moore, CJS: Asked about growth drivers, divestitures, and margin expansion. Etzkorn projected about 3% organic growth, with $75 million in potential divestitures, and margin gains from cost structure improvements. Lippert highlighted facility consolidations and margin resilience.
  • Moore, CJS: Questioned aftermarket ramp and margin goals. Lippert cited proprietary parts and share gains, while Etzkorn noted near-term margin pressure from ongoing investments.
  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: Asked about industry outlook shifts and Q1 sales trends. Lippert cited cautious dealer behavior and weather as factors.
  • Tristan Thomas-Martin, BMO: Probed on dealer hesitancy, trade-up cycle, and impact of entry-level RVs. Lippert described a 3–5 year trade cycle and expected quicker trade-ins for smaller units.
  • Brandon Rollé, Loop Capital: Inquired about affordability and pricing. Lippert discussed OEM focus on reducing ASPs and noted aluminum costs as a headwind.
  • Kevin Condon, Baird: Sought details on margin drivers and product mix. Lippert and Etzkorn emphasized facility consolidation and operating leverage. Single axle mix was 21% in Q4, with fifth wheels strong.
  • Michael Albanese, Benchmark: Asked about RV product mix expectations. Lippert observed high-end buyers are more active, with single axle demand easing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but pressed on retail trends, dealer discipline, and margin cadence, with skepticism around early-year sales and margin ramp.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, stating "we are confident in our strategy" and focusing on structural improvements, while acknowledging ongoing industry headwinds and dealer caution. Confidence was consistent with the previous quarter, though current responses included more explicit references to industry challenges and operational discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management shifted guidance for RV shipments to 335,000–350,000 units for 2026, slightly narrowing from the previous quarter's 345,000–360,000 range.
  • Operating margin target for 2026 increased to 7.5%–8% from the previous guidance of 7%–8%.
  • More emphasis was placed on facility consolidations (8–10 planned in 2026 versus 5 executed in 2025), and the strategy to divest $75 million in lower-margin products was reiterated.
  • Analysts continued to focus on dealer inventory, product mix, and aftermarket ramp, with more questions about margin cadence and the impact of macroeconomic factors.
  • Management tone remained confident but more focused on market discipline and incremental growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited headwinds from higher aluminum costs and material tariffs, which pressured aftermarket margins.
  • Dealer caution and weather-related disruptions impacted retail trends and Q1 sales growth.
  • Ongoing investments in aftermarket infrastructure are expected to create near-term margin pressure.
  • The company acknowledged the potential risk of slower retail recovery due to small and midsize dealer struggles and overall industry caution.

Final Takeaway

LCI Industries closed 2025 with robust year-over-year growth and expanded margins, supported by innovation, market share gains, and operational efficiencies. The company is targeting 2026 revenue of $4.2 billion to $4.3 billion, operating margin of 7.5% to 8%, and adjusted EPS of $8.25 to $9.25, with growth driven by OEM and aftermarket strategies, facility consolidations, and select divestitures. Management remains confident in its ability to navigate industry headwinds and deliver shareholder value through further cost discipline and product innovation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: JetBlue pleased with Q1 performance, sees no meaningful impact from WS Fern
data.description:
JetBlue (JBLU) management said they are “super pleased” with the carrier’s performance in the first quarter and dismissed any meaningful impact from last month’s Winter Storm Fern despite widespread delays and cancellations.

Speaking at the Barclays 43rd Annual Industrial Select Conference, management said achieving break even or better operating margin is the company’s “number one goal,” ultimately leading to positive free cash flow by 2027. 

JetBlue (JBLU) believes this goal can be reached with 3.5% revenue per available seat mile ((RASM)) growth and cost per available seat mile ((CASM)) limited to 2%.

These targets mirror the company’s guidance in their most recent quarterly results, with CASM-ex-fuel forecasted to be between 1.0% and 3.0% in FY26, while adjusted operating margin was expected to at least break even or better.

Separately, JetBlue (JBLU) is adding twice-daily non-stop flights between Houston and New York. Starting May 21, two flights per day will fly nonstop between Houston’s George Bush Intercontinental Airport and New York’s JFK International Airport.

data.title: Older Americans, on average, watched more Winter Olympics on Peacock
data.description:
According to a report by data analytics provider Apptopia, older Americans have spent 90 minutes on average watching the 2026 Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics on their smartphones.

In the first 10 days of streaming the Winter Olympics on Peacock (CMCSA), viewership on mobile compared to previous Olympic events was lower, but the watch time of the audience was longer, the report said.

"Comparing the past three Olympic Games on Peacock, Average Time Spent per DAU is up for all age groups for these Winter Games, with the exception of the 36-45 bracket," Apptopia said. "Older users, those aged 46+, saw the largest increase over the Summer 2024 games, at 48.5%. They are watching more than 90 minutes of Olympic coverage a day on their phones."

When compared to the Summer Olympics, daily active users for the Winter event are down 9%.

However, the surge in DAUs from Day 1 to Day 10 was a whopping 92% for the Winter Olympics this year, which is much higher than the 35% rise for Winter 2022 and the 27% jump for Summer 2024 for the same duration.

Apptopia found that about 27% of users who watched the Winter Olympics on Peacock were new and had never had a session on the app before, and about 23% were "resurrected users," or those who had "churned" but re-engaged for the event. The remaining 50% were already active users on the streaming platform.

"This means the Winter Games functioned less as a pure acquisition play and more as a full-funnel lifecycle tool, reactivating lapsed subscribers while deepening engagement among the existing base," said Apptopia Director Adam Blacker.

More on Comcast

data.title: Eni weighs return to oil and gas trading, seeking rivals' big profits - FT
data.description:
Eni (E) is considering a return to oil and gas trading, as it seeks the billions in returns enjoyed by European rivals BP, Shell and TotalEnergies, the Financial Times reported Wednesday.

"I stopped trading in 2019 but the other big companies are all traders," Eni (E) CEO Claudio Descalzi told FT. "BP, Shell, Total are big traders and they make billions from that."

Descalzi also said Eni (E) is in preliminary talks with a number of commodity trading houses, including Mercuria, about forming a joint venture.

"It is not in our DNA. We are not very commercial," the CEO said. "So I thought to become commercial, we have to have a partnership to understand the business... If we can offer physical hedging, that is a big advantage for [a partner]. We can complement each other," he said, adding the amount of oil and gas that Eni (E) produces should make it an attractive partner.

Despite oil trading's profits for the major European firms, BP and Shell both noted weaker performances in their trading units over the final three months of last year.

data.title: ‘Don’t abandon U.S. markets, but have exposure in Europe’ – strategist
data.description:
Anastasia Amoroso, chief investment strategist at Partners Group, is urging investors to maintain their U.S. equity positions (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) while building exposure to European markets (IEV), (IEUR), (SPEU), citing unprecedented steps by European leaders to boost economic resilience.

In an interview with CNBC, Amoroso pointed to Europe’s commitment to defense spending, new trade partnerships, and regional economic strength as reasons the continent deserves more than a passing glance from investors.

While acknowledging that U.S. markets have been “churning” compared to the rally in international stocks, Amoroso said the domestic picture remains solid. “The fundamentals of the U.S. economy are actually quite robust,” she explained, attributing recent consolidation to a “rolling AI scare” rather than any structural weakness.

The strategist highlighted several new growth engines emerging in Europe. Germany has committed to spending 5% of GDP on defense, driving industrial goods orders higher, while the European Union recently struck what Amoroso called a “monster deal” with India that cuts tariffs on 96% of European goods heading to the world’s fifth-largest economy.

Amoroso also noted a divergence between Northern and Southern Europe that provides additional stability. Countries like Spain, Portugal, and Italy have more consumer and services-oriented economies, which helps insulate them from export disruptions related to China.

When asked about concerns that regulatory burdens could stifle Europe’s growth potential, Amoroso acknowledged the issue but expressed optimism.

“The Draghi proposal is the reason why we see sort of this buildup in resilience in Europe,” she said, noting that tangible actions by European leaders are encouraging even if ultimate success remains uncertain.

The current macro environment, Amoroso argued, favors geographical diversification across multiple regions rather than concentration in any single market.

“Don’t abandon the U.S., but have exposure to Europe as well,” she advised, emphasizing that localization and supply chain shifts make broad international exposure essential for investors.

data.title: J&J to build over $1B cell therapy production site in Pennsylvania
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) on Wednesday announced plans to construct a cell therapy manufacturing facility in Pennsylvania at an investment of more than $1B, further boosting its business operations in the state.

The new facility to be built in Montgomery County, PA, is part of J&J’s (JNJ) efforts to spend $55B by 2029 to expand its presence in manufacturing, R&D, and technology in the U.S.

The investment is expected to generate over 500 biomanufacturing jobs and more than 4,000 construction jobs for the local economy, according to the company.

Backed by 10 facilities spanning over 2M square feet, J&J (JNJ) runs manufacturing, R&D, and office operations in Pennsylvania, adding roughly $10B to the state annually.

“For 140 years, Johnson & Johnson has been a leading innovator in American healthcare, and we are honored to continue advancing that legacy in Pennsylvania,” CEO Joaquin Duato remarked.

data.title: McDonald's attracts a bull rating from Erste Group
data.description:
Erste Group boosted its rating on McDonald's Corporation (MCD) to Buy from Hold on Wednesday.

The Austra-based research firm expects McDonald's (MCD) sales will grow more strongly again in 2026 than in the previous year. "McDonald's has a very high and stable operating margin, which is well above that of its competitors," highlighted the analyst team.

In terms of valuation, McDonald's (MCD) P/E ratio was noted to be around the sector average. However, due to the company's high profitability and good prospects, potential is seen for the multiple to stretch out and for the share price to rise further.

Wall Street analysts have a consensus Buy rating on McDonald's (MCD), while Seeking Alpha analysts are more cautious with a consensus Hold rating in place.

Shares of McDonald's (MCD) are up 7.2% on a year-to-date basis.
